steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence (Quantitative) question. The underlined claim says divided Congress is a "necessary but not sufficient condition" for decreasing government size (total outlays). "Necessary" means every reduction must occur under a divided Congress. "Not sufficient" means a divided Congress does not always produce a reduction. The correct data choice must demonstrate both patterns using total outlays only.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'undivided Congresses increased nondefense outlays; divided Congresses except one reduced defense outlays': The claim is exclusively about total outlays, not the nondefense/defense breakdown. This uses the wrong columns. ✗
  • Choice B — 'all divided Congresses reduced total outlays, though two also increased nondefense outlays': Says ALL divided Congresses produced reductions — that would make divided Congress sufficient, contradicting the "not sufficient" part of the claim. ✗
  • Choice C — 'undivided Congresses increased total outlays; divided Congresses reduced nondefense or defense outlays': Again refers to the nondefense/defense breakdown rather than total outlays, which is what Hanke's claim is about. ✗
  • Choice D — 'all divided Congresses except one reduced total outlays; undivided Congresses increased total outlays': Shows necessity (all reductions happened under divided Congresses, since undivided Congresses always increased) and non-sufficiency (the one exception — a divided Congress that did not produce a reduction). Uses total outlays only. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
